name: Ashten Barber
title: A Financial Visionary Redefining Wealth Mindsets
story:
For over a decade, Ashten Barber has been a guiding force in the financial industry, helping clients navigate economic cycles, life transitions, and personal financial growth. As the founder of Eclipse Financial Advisors, her company is built on the principle that financial markets, like eclipses, are cyclical and predictable. Yet, while the market may follow patterns, every client’s financial journey is uniquely shaped by their life experiences.

Ashten’s approach to finance is deeply personal. She doesn’t just look at numbers, she looks at lives. Whether working with a 20-year-old just starting out, a middle-aged entrepreneur building wealth, or a widow in her late 70s adjusting to life without her spouse, she tailors her guidance to each individual’s reality.

Ashten’s financial mindset was shaped early on. Raised with the belief that money is a privilege rather than a burden, she learned from her mother’s disciplined approach to credit. Her mother secured her first credit card at a time when women had to prove financial responsibility before being approved. This instilled in Ashten a deep respect for financial management.

She recalls being shocked as an adult when she discovered that people carried balances on their credit cards. “I had my first child before I found out that you could actually carry a balance. That blew my mind.” To her, debt was not a tool but credit was. This perspective, coupled with a habit of meticulously tracking finances, became the foundation of her career.

Interestingly, Ashten never set out to become a financial expert. Her original dream was to be an actress. But after spending a year on stage and rarely seeing her soon-to-be husband, she realized that lifestyle wasn’t for her. A surprising turn came when they did their first tax return together and she found the process fascinating.

Initially, she pursued accounting but quickly realized she wasn’t a “paper person” — instead she was a “people person.” Finance was the perfect balance, allowing her to blend numbers with human connection. Though her father initially resisted her career change, believing she was giving up her true passion, her mother’s discovery of personal finance books among her teenage romance novels proved otherwise.

One of the most common misconceptions Ashten encounters is that financial advisors are only for the wealthy. In reality, she believes financial planning is for everyone. She has worked with clients who spent years fearing financial instability, only to discover they had hidden wealth they never realized they could use.

Her philosophy revolves around three key principles: Start with planning; Build trust and clarity; Think generationally. She identifies two primary mistakes people make with money: letting emotions dictate decisions and being too afraid to start. Her number one tip: be honest with yourself.

Balancing work and family, she prioritizes quality time with her children and makes space for self-care. Her advice to entrepreneurs is to be 100% sold on what you do — passion fuels persistence.

Ashten Barber’s journey exemplifies perseverance, adaptability, and financial empowerment. Her ultimate advice: trust your intuition, block out external noise, and live life on your own terms.

name: Ama Ramos Pipim
title: Blossoming Resilience: Ama’s Paper Flower Odyssey
story:
Ama Ramos Pipim’s journey is a testament to resilience, creativity, and the power of self-discovery. As a Ghanaian-American paper flower artist, her path to entrepreneurship wasn't straightforward, but it was deeply meaningful. From struggling with postpartum depression to rediscovering her creative spark through paper flowers, Ama's story is one of transformation and perseverance.

The defining moment came when Ama, faced with the challenge of decorating her daughter's birthday party on a tight budget, discovered paper flowers on Pinterest. Despite initially feeling discouraged by the high price point, her sister's encouragement reignited her creative passion. With determination and support, Ama delved into the art of paper flower crafting, finding solace and joy in the process.

As she honed her craft over the years, Ama's hobby evolved into a business: Wild Fleur Blooms, rooted in love and blossoming with joy. However, the journey wasn't without its obstacles. The onset of the pandemic disrupted her plans, but it also sparked a period of artistic growth and innovation. Ama leaned towards creating realistic paper flowers and embraced sustainable alternatives, further enriching her business.

Balancing motherhood and entrepreneurship posed its own challenges, but Ama's dedication to her children never wavered. She learned to prioritize family time amidst her busy schedule, ensuring her daughters felt loved and included in her journey. Through it all, Ama's resilience shone brightly, inspiring others to persevere in the face of adversity.

Ama's story is a reminder that success isn't always linear, but it's deeply rewarding when fueled by passion, resilience, and a supportive community. From struggling with self-doubt to finding her stride as an entrepreneur, Ama's journey embodies the transformative power of chasing one's dreams with unwavering determination and grace.

name: Bonnie Christine
title: Bonnie Christine: A Journey from Mom to Million-Dollar Surface Pattern Designer
story:
In the enchanting world of surface pattern design, Bonnie Christine stands as a testament to the transformative power of passion and determination. A proud mom, Bonnie introduces herself not just as a surface pattern designer but as someone deeply connected to the artistry of creating beautiful designs inspired by the delicate elegance of flowers.

Bonnie's journey into the realm of surface pattern design wasn't a typical one. Her entrepreneurial spirit led her to venture into online classes, where she shared her profound love for designing fabrics, papers, wallpapers, and more. Graduating from business school, Bonnie initially collaborated with her mother in a quilt store. It was amidst the charming bolts of fabrics that the realization struck her – behind those mesmerizing designs was a creator, and she, too, harbored the desire to be a designer.

Despite lacking a background in design or business, Bonnie's unwavering dream propelled her forward. She contemplated the possibility, slept on the idea, and over the course of a few months, she convinced herself that if others could turn design into a livelihood, so could she. With that fathom, Bonnie embarked on her journey as a surface pattern designer, dedicating herself to daily actions that moved her closer to realizing her dream.

Two years into her journey, Bonnie achieved a significant milestone – signing a fabric licensing contract with Art Gallery Fabrics. Her primary focus was to evolve and flourish as a designer, yet life had a surprise in store for her. A request from someone to learn Adobe Illustrator marked the inception of her teaching journey. Proficient in the software, Bonnie readily agreed to share her knowledge.

As Bonnie delved into teaching, she discovered more than just a means to impart skills; she found remarkable stories among her students. Women from diverse corners of the world, seeking 'permission' to explore their creativity, resonated with Bonnie's teaching. This realization became the catalyst for Bonnie to contribute to the artist community. Her goal was to make creative pursuits accessible to everyone, removing the notion of 'starving artists' from her vocabulary.

Thus, Bonnie initiated her comprehensive surface design course, 'Immersion.' Rooted in the belief of community over competition, Bonnie aimed to create a supportive environment where artists could uplift and encourage each other. For her, surface designing wasn't just a craft; it was happiness – the ability to gather inspiration from the world and craft something beautiful that adds to the world's beauty.

Bonnie Christine's commitment to supporting her fellow artists has yielded not only immense satisfaction but also a flourishing business worth millions. Her story is not just one of personal success but a genuine contribution to the global community of artists. She stands tall, advocating the idea that the journey of surface designing is a journey of joy and fulfillment, where passion and consistency can lead to both personal and professional success.

In Bonnie's world, the creation of art isn't just about the end product but also about fostering a community where creativity thrives, and artists uplift each other, proving that, indeed, the beauty of art lies not only in the designs but in the connections and collaborations that flourish within the artistic community.

name: Chelsea Espinoza
title: How Chelsea Espinoza Turned Healing Into Her Life’s Purpose
story:
For many women, the journey toward a better life rarely begins with a dramatic moment. More often, it starts quietly — through exhaustion we normalize, dreams we postpone, or discomfort we learn to ignore. For Chelsea Espinoza, that quiet discomfort eventually became impossible to overlook, and it changed the direction of her life entirely.

Today, Chelsea is the founder of Road to Jubilee, a purpose-driven business dedicated to helping women create fuller, healthier, and more meaningful lives through coaching, leadership development, workshops, and speaking engagements. But before Road to Jubilee became a company, it began as something far more personal — a promise born from hardship, healing, and courage.

Ask Chelsea what Road to Jubilee means, and her answer goes far beyond business.

“Road to Jubilee is my company, but more than that, it’s my purpose,” she says. “I help women improve their quality of life.”

For Chelsea, quality of life is not a trendy phrase or vague aspiration. It is deeply personal and different for every individual. To one person, it may mean freedom. To another, peace. To someone else, confidence or balance. What matters, she believes, is helping women reconnect with the parts of themselves they often sacrifice while caring for everyone else.

For years, Chelsea lived that reality herself.

She poured energy into work, relationships, responsibilities, and daily demands while rarely making space for herself. Eventually, the cost became unavoidable.

Several years ago, Chelsea experienced severe and unexplained health issues that disrupted every aspect of her life. Despite countless tests and appointments, doctors could not identify the cause.

“I was sick, out of work for months, and uncertain if I would live,” she recalls. “I knew I never wanted to be in that place again.”

What followed was not simply recovery — it was transformation.

During the healing process, Chelsea began asking herself difficult questions that many people avoid: What truly matters? What would a nourishing life actually look like? How do you build a life you no longer need to escape from?

The answers slowly became the foundation of Road to Jubilee.

“This business is me living on purpose,” she explains. “It’s me taking action on the second chance I was given.”

Long before the business officially existed, the vision had already taken shape.

Chelsea spent years imagining a retreat space dedicated to restoration, connection, and community — especially for women. Whenever she described the vision, she noticed the same reaction: women immediately connected with the idea of having space to simply breathe.

She imagined gardens, shared meals, meaningful conversations, fresh produce harvested by hand, and environments where women could finally exhale.

“The retreat property will be called Jubilee,” she says. “I don’t know when or where it will exist yet, but I know the path I’m on leads there.”

After recovering and returning to her position as vice president of a landscape construction company, another realization became impossible to ignore.

She had regained her health, but she still wasn’t where she was meant to be.

During a quiet vacation away from daily pressures, she experienced something unfamiliar — complete calm.

“No anxiety. No hesitation,” she says. “I knew that I knew that I knew. It was time.”

Chelsea’s transition into entrepreneurship wasn’t entirely new territory. Before entering construction leadership, she had already worked as an interior designer and business owner. Each experience contributed valuable lessons about operations, leadership, and human behavior.

Even so, building a purpose-driven company came with entirely different challenges.

“I gave myself a month to decompress,” she explains. “To listen. To ask myself: What am I building? Who am I building this for?”

Starting again in a completely different industry proved both exciting and humbling. While friends and colleagues immediately supported her vision, earning trust from clients required patience.

“What surprised me most was how long trust-building takes,” she says. “This work is personal. It’s delicate. People have to feel safe with you.”

Still, Chelsea believes discomfort is often evidence of growth.

“There’s an uncomfortable growth spurt when you choose a new path,” she reflects. “You question everything — am I doing the right thing? Am I enough? But those questions aren’t a stop sign. They’re part of the journey.”

When asked about the transformations she witnesses, Chelsea struggles to choose only one story.

“Every woman I meet reminds me why I do this,” she says. “Every single day.”

Her clients often arrive during periods of transition — navigating burnout, career changes, identity shifts, or rediscovering parts of themselves they lost along the way.

One client in particular remains unforgettable: a woman who had always dreamed of living somewhere specific but never believed it could become reality.

“She did all the work,” Chelsea says. “But watching her step into the life she thought would never happen was incredible.”

Chelsea Espinoza’s journey is ultimately a story about reclaiming ownership — over health, purpose, identity, and possibility. Through Road to Jubilee, she continues helping women discover something many have forgotten: creating a meaningful life is not selfish. Sometimes, it is the most important work we can do.

name: Diana Edwards
title: Inner Compass with Life’s Purpose: The Journey of Diana Edwards
story:
In the heart of Denver, Colorado, where the April snowstorms dance with the promises of spring, resides a woman of remarkable versatility and resilience - Diana Edwards. Her story weaves through the corridors of education, entrepreneurship, and self-discovery, illuminating the profound impact of following one's passions and embracing life's unexpected turns.

Diana's educational odyssey is a testament to the power of exploration and adaptation. Raised in a small town where the echoes of scientific pursuit reverberated through the community, she was initially steered towards the realm of engineering. Fuelled by familial expectations, she embarked on a path adorned with gears and equations, earning degrees in mechanical and biomedical engineering. Yet, the whispers of her true calling lingered in the recesses of her heart, nurtured by love for sports and a deep-seated curiosity about the human body.

It was amidst the rhythm of her running strides and the silent symphony of her weightlifting sessions that Diana discovered her passion for wellness and healing. The transition from engineering to physical therapy was not merely a change of profession but a profound alignment of her inner compass with the trajectory of her life's purpose. Driven by a desire to make a positive impact on others' health, she embarked on a journey that would redefine her path and illuminate the interconnectedness of mind, body, and spirit.

As a physical therapist, Diana embodies the ethos of holistic healing, recognizing the symbiotic relationship between physical and mental well-being. In the tapestry of her practice, she integrates the threads of nutrition, sleep, and emotional resilience, advocating for a comprehensive approach to wellness. Her empathy, forged through her own battles with anxiety and chronic pain, serves as a beacon of hope for those navigating the labyrinth of physical and mental health challenges.

Beyond the confines of her clinical domain, Diana ventures into the realm of entrepreneurship, infusing creativity and craftsmanship into her endeavors. Through her Etsy shop, "Diana's Sewing Creations," she channels her passion for sewing into a tapestry of oven mitts, skillet covers, and aprons. What began as a creative outlet during the course of the pandemic evolved into a flourishing venture, fueled by a deep-seated passion and a commitment to quality craftsmanship.

In the maze of entrepreneurship, Diana navigates the ebb and flow of success with grace and resilience. She embraces the lessons of patience, perseverance, and passion, recognizing that true fulfillment transcends the boundaries of profit margins and sales figures. Her North Star, a guiding light amidst life's uncertainties, emanates from the depths of her core values - family, authenticity, and self-care.

As Diana continues to tread the winding paths of her journey, she remains steadfast in her commitment to authenticity and self-discovery. With each step, she unveils new layers of strength and wisdom, embracing the myriad hues of her existence with grace and gratitude. In her story, we find echoes of our own journeys - a testament to the transformative power of embracing our passions.

In life's tapestry, Diana Edwards shines bright. Through her work, she embodies strength, authenticity, and determination. Let's find inspiration in her story as we explore our own journeys.

name: Jan Miller
title: Designing What Lasts
story: By the time Jan Miller talks about design, it becomes clear he isn’t simply describing a career — he’s describing a legacy, a mindset, and a way of understanding the world. The award-winning 21-13 Impact Graphics officially launched in 2005, but its story began much earlier. Growing up around the print industry while watching his father operate Royal Paper Box for decades, Jan was immersed in conversations about ink, paper stocks, finishes, and production long before most people understood what design actually meant. Long before design became digital-first, Jan learned something many still overlook today: what works on a screen doesn’t always work in print — and understanding the difference matters. At the time, Jan worked for a DVD production company during the peak of the Blu-ray versus HD era. Initially focused on video editing and programming, client requests slowly shifted his role. Businesses needed DVD packaging, posters, print materials, and marketing assets. Jan became the natural choice. Eventually, he wasn’t editing videos anymore. He was building visual identities. Then came the moment many entrepreneurs recognize only in hindsight. The company’s financial struggles became increasingly obvious. Invoices weren’t being collected, operations were slowing, and Jan could see what was coming. When he resigned, management asked him not to tell clients. It didn’t matter. The clients already knew who they trusted. Within days, vendors and customers were reaching out directly. Three major companies followed immediately — not because of contracts, but because of relationships. 21-13 Impact Graphics was born, built not on marketing hype, but on trust and results. Surviving Change and Reinventing the Business Like many entrepreneurs, Jan faced his first major test during the 2008 financial crisis. The independent film market collapsed. Studios reduced spending. Blockbuster stopped purchasing smaller films. Revenue dropped dramatically. Instead of panicking, Jan adapted. He expanded beyond entertainment into branding, logos, marketing, corporate design, websites, and promotional materials while continuing to serve entertainment clients through the industry's transition from physical media to streaming. Today, more than 40% of 21-13 Impact Graphics still operates within entertainment — now creating assets for streaming platforms rather than DVD shelves. The company’s evolution reflects the principle Jan believes matters most: adaptability. A Changing Industry, A Constant Discipline For Jan, graphic design resembles medicine in one important way: if you stop learning, you fall behind. Software evolves constantly. Tools change. Workflows become obsolete. Tasks that once required hours can now be completed within minutes — if you understand the tools properly. When Canva disrupted the industry, Jan didn’t reject it. He contextualized it. Canva works well for quick projects and simple content, but businesses often fail to recognize what they sacrifice: ownership, scalability, print accuracy, and production quality. Then AI arrived. Like every major technological shift, AI introduced fear — fear of replacement, irrelevance, and disappearing opportunities. Jan sees it differently. To him, AI is simply another tool. Powerful? Yes. Complete? No. AI can generate images, but it doesn’t understand embroidery requirements, production specifications, layered assets, or printing realities. Professionals do. Jan uses AI selectively — assembling, refining, directing, and improving outputs rather than treating automation as a finished product. “The goal isn’t automation,” he explains. “It’s amplification.” His advice for emerging designers remains straightforward: learn fundamentals first, understand AI, learn Canva well enough to fix client files — but build expertise around real skills. Riding Economic Waves The design industry has always been cyclical. Busy seasons arrive. Slow seasons follow. Markets shift. Politics changes. What matters, Jan believes, is staying fluid. When client work slows, he works on the business itself — improving websites, refining messaging, updating systems, and preparing for future opportunities. He also warns against over-specialization. Entertainment alone wasn’t enough during strikes and downturns. Diversification kept the business healthy. “The work exists,” Jan says. “But it doesn’t come to you. You go get it.” Networking remains non-negotiable. Jan teaches a simple rule: maintain one closed networking group, one open networking group, and one social network. More than that often creates burnout. But networking alone isn’t enough. Relationships require follow-up. Businesses rarely grow passively. Solo, Not Alone After seeing partnerships succeed and fail repeatedly, Jan approaches them cautiously. Partnerships can work — but only with clear legal structures, difficult conversations, and contingency plans. Solo ownership creates different challenges but offers clarity. Decisions move faster. Vision remains focused. For Jan, award-winning businesses aren’t built through perfection. They’re built through consistency. Listening carefully. Choosing battles wisely. Understanding that sometimes getting paid matters more than proving yourself right. He pays attention not only to what clients say — but what they unintentionally reveal. Those hidden details often become the foundation of strong brands. Creativity Meets Craft One of Jan’s greatest strengths is understanding his limitations. He hires specialists. Developers. Bookkeepers. Experts. Even when capable of doing everything himself, he chooses not to. Because building businesses requires trust. Creatives work long hours. Late nights are normal. But creativity alone doesn’t build companies. Structure does. Meetings matter. Networking matters. Showing up intentionally matters. A New Chapter: The Glass Leash Jan’s newest venture combines technology, emotion, and artistry. What started with portraits of his own dog evolved into The Glass Leash — a business transforming everyday pet photos into premium glass-mounted artwork. Each image undergoes careful refinement: backgrounds cleaned, details reconstructed, lighting improved, and emotional elements enhanced. Printed directly onto glass, the final pieces require no frames — producing vibrant, modern artwork designed to preserve meaningful memories. Although pet portraits remain the focus, Jan also creates memorial pieces, recognizing the emotional importance these images often carry. It isn’t simply photography. It’s storytelling frozen in glass. Jan’s Message “Research. Take notes. Learn from companies doing it well. Network online and in person. If you think launching a website is enough, good luck. You have to reach out. You have to talk to people. That’s how businesses are built.” After more than two decades in design, Jan Miller hasn’t remained relevant by resisting change. He has stayed relevant by staying curious. And in an industry defined by constant evolution, that may be the most timeless design principle of all.

name: Karen Brown
title: Karen Brown’s Road to Mastery with ‘Just Get It Done Quilts’
story:
Karen Brown's journey from running a successful business to becoming a prominent figure in the quilting and YouTube community is nothing short of inspiring. Her brand, 'Just Get It Done Quilts,' embodies her ethos of perseverance, creativity, and dedication.

Before the inception of 'Just Get It Done Quilts,' Karen balanced two full-time roles: running a successful appliance business with her husband and being a devoted mother to four children. Despite the demanding nature of her previous endeavors, Karen's passion for quilting and creating content led her to YouTube, where she embarked on a journey filled with challenges and triumphs.

Karen's entrance to YouTube was not without its hurdles. She candidly shares her initial struggles with filming and editing, highlighting the steep learning curve she encountered. However, her determination to deliver quality content propelled her forward, ultimately shaping her into the seasoned content creator she is today. It’s a culmination of hundreds of small things.

Behind the polished videos and vibrant newsletters lies an immense amount of effort, time, and dedication. Karen provides insight into the meticulous planning, crafting, scripting, and editing processes that each video undergoes, emphasizing the commitment required to maintain a consistent and engaging online presence.

In navigating the realm of technology, Karen reassures aspiring creators that a smartphone is more than sufficient to kickstart their journey. She dispels the notion of immediate success, emphasizing the importance of perseverance and continuous improvement.

Consistency, a cornerstone of content creation, is approached with subtlety by Karen. While regular posting is beneficial, she stresses the significance of delivering quality content that resonates with viewers, rather than adhering to a rigid posting schedule dictated by algorithms.

Sponsorship and product promotion present new opportunities for creators, yet Karen acknowledges the challenges associated with maintaining authenticity amidst commercial endeavors. She prioritizes alignment with products and companies she genuinely believes in, preserving the integrity of her brand.

Karen's journey is deeply rooted in family support and entrepreneurial legacy. Drawing inspiration from her grandmother's entrepreneurial spirit and her mother's multifaceted ventures, Karen navigates the complexities of entrepreneurship with resilience and grace.

Financial freedom, to Karen, signifies the ability to pursue her passions and make meaningful contributions to her community. She emphasizes the importance of finding a niche and cultivating a loyal audience, underscoring the transformative power of online platforms in amplifying one's voice and impact.

Navigating the landscape of social media, Karen recognizes the evolving preferences of audiences and the diverse opportunities for creators. She encourages authenticity and innovation, urging individuals to carve out their unique path amidst a crowded digital landscape.

Karen's approach to handling feedback, both positive and negative, reflects her commitment to fostering a supportive and inclusive community. She emphasizes the importance of constructive dialogue and boundary-setting, prioritizing the well-being of both creators and audiences.

In closing, Karen imparts invaluable advice for aspiring entrepreneurs, emphasizing the journey of self-discovery, strength of character, and adaptation. Through her journey, Karen exemplifies the transformative power of passion, perseverance, and community in achieving success in the digital age.

name: Karnam Malleswari
title: Triumph Over Adversity
story:
In the humble town of Andhra Pradesh, India, Karnam Malleswari, the golden girl, emerged from a modest family of five siblings. Her father served as a constable at Indian Railways, and her mother diligently tended to the household. Malleswari's journey to greatness began when her sister entered weightlifting, allowing Karnam to accompany her to the training center and witness the world of strength training.

Initially, Karnam found joy in observing the training sessions, but destiny had different plans. Intrigued by the sport, she approached the coach seeking training for herself. However, that moment changed her life forever. The coach, assessing the seemingly frail 12-year-old girl, remarked that she was not suited to become a weightlifter and would be better helping her mother with household work.

Those words deeply hurt Malleswari and ignited a determination to prove everyone wrong. She realized she did not need others’ opinions to understand her potential. She found an abandoned gym nearby and started practicing barefoot using whatever limited equipment she could find.

Her sister's ascent to the national level became a pivotal moment. During selection trials for the 1990 Asian Games, Karnam Malleswari carefully observed participants from Russia, eager to learn their techniques. Indian team coach Genedy Rebecon recognized her dedication and, despite her lacking official qualifications, took her under his guidance.

A year of rigorous training and unwavering determination enabled Malleswari to break national records and secure gold medals. She immediately began preparing for international championships, especially the 1995 World Weightlifting Championships in China.

Malleswari made up her mind to win gold because she dreamed of hearing the Indian National Anthem played in her honor. Driven by sheer willpower, Karnam not only won three gold medals but also created a world record.

Ironically, despite her victory, the audio recording of the Indian National Anthem had not been submitted to the committee and therefore could not be played during the award ceremony. Nevertheless, Padma Shri awardee Dr. Karnam Malleswari stood proudly on stage, holding her medal and the Indian flag high, while singing the National Anthem herself.

name: Kevin O’Leary
title: Business tips from the Shark Excerpt from podcast Erika Taught Me, interviewing Kevin O’Leary
story: Kevin O’Leary is a businessman, investor and Shark Tank star. In this episode, Kevin talks about his relationship with money, what it takes to be a good leader, and how he finds good talent for his companies. He also shares advice on investing, what his current portfolio allocation looks like, and gives advice on how anyone can make their first $100,000. Kevin O’Leary begins by crediting his mentor Jerry Patterson, recalling what Patterson had told him years ago: “if things go well, a lot of things will be thrown at you. You are going to hear a lot of noise, criticism, and all kinds of things from every angle every day and successful managers will be able to focus on the goal and shut off all the noise.” So O’Leary has been following a pattern of focusing on three things he wants to get done every day. Even though there are a lot of things going on a daily basis, he focuses on three paramount things for a particular day. He chooses not to heed to the noise and criticism which is irrelevant to achieving his goal. He says that if he achieves his goal it helps a lot of people since he is a businessman, an investor. He and his team assess every situation as either an opportunity, or a catastrophe and focus their energy in serving the opportunities. When shooting a television commercial for customer acquisition, using a remarkable fusion of art and technology, he relates to the importance of quality of work that he used to follow when he used to be a cameraman with 60mm film and editing tools. He keeps editing even today with Premiere Pro editor just to keep his chops. If he notices a jump frame or something that doesn’t look great in the commercial, he wants to fix it until it is perfect. His routine is to get up at 5 every morning, get on the bike as soon as the Sun breaks the horizon on the ocean, ride for about nine miles, go to the gym for 40 mins and be back on the saddle by 7: 45 and get started with work for the day. O’Leary explains that one has to be careful in settling for less. He emphasizes that there is no gray area in the financial services industry; either you make money or you lose it. The hardest job for any entrepreneur is to prioritize what is important, because that’s the real skill of managing and not wasting time on unimportant things. Managers that fail are the ones who try to make everyone happy all the time because it is impossible to please everyone. O’Leary expounds that someone had told him that it was impossible to make his first million dollars and he agrees that it was really hard to earn that first million dollars, then it was impossible to make his first five million dollars, again he acknowledges that they were right and it was indeed very hard to make that five million dollars. And then it wasn’t so hard anymore, since he started to figure out what works and what doesn't. He has had a lot of success and a lot of failures too. Today, he says that he doesn't need any more money and he’s been fortunate enough to have the wealth he own, but what he really likes is to have more time. Hence, he has realized that his most valuable asset today is how he utilizes his time. You could either use the time available wisely or waste it. “The whole reason for becoming an entrepreneur is not for the greed of money, it's about the pursuit of personal freedom; that’s what it’s about, to be able to do whatever you want with your time, the most valuable asset,” O’Leary lays out. He explains that he chops up each day into 30 minute blocks. At the end of the day, he reviews for the next day, and he has the freedom to discard a task if he doesn’t feel like doing it. He says he wishes that kind of success for everybody so that they will be able to control their own white space of time. O’Leary is a wrist watch collector and he is interested in new brands and what they have to offer. When someone has money, they can take risks. He advises his young entrepreneurs and CEOs that they have to get to the point that they have five million dollars in the bank because they can survive the rest of their lives no matter what happens and will also be able to take care of others with 5 - 6 percent returns from $5 million.  When they make that five million dollars, they have to keep it aside and not risk it. Thereafter, they can take a bit of risk, since people will know what works and what doesn’t. That’s why some are extremely successful while some are catastrophic failures. Fortunately, his success surpasses setbacks and his motivation endures, the only reason he keeps working, he says, is because he can buy more watches. He goes on explaining his own success story. While many think it’s an overnight success, the reality is quite different. His journey started with a dream of becoming a photographer or a cinematographer. O’Leary expressed his desire to his father. His father opposed the idea and warned him that he would starve to death competing with great photographers, cinematographers and so many great editors, breaking through the competition would be impossible. Obviously, O’Leary was disappointed and replied to his dad that he was not being supportive. Guided by his father, who recognized his unfamiliarity with the field, he was advised to complete school, pursue University education and ultimately attain a Master’s degree in Business Administration. This comprehensive approach equipped O’Leary with necessary skills for entrepreneurship, allowing him the flexibility to choose any direction he desired. As soon as he got out of business school per plan he started his first company that made short films for the original six hockey teams for the networks. He and his team would hussle all week shooting with Hockey players, teams and coaches in Detroit, New York, Toronto and so on. O’Leary would edit the films and send them to the satellite to appear on Saturday Hockey games. Then he started producing Don Cherry’s Grapevine, a podcast format which talks about hockey and more. He barely knew what a format was, at the time. A format is an ownership, a type of Intellectual Property in Television. He also produced another program called The Original Six. In a couple of years, the network started knocking on the doors, saying that they wanted to buy the company. O’Leary was thrilled to understand the strategy of running a business. “Oh this is how it works!” he exclaims. He sold that company and moved on to his next project. O’Leary, along with John Freeman, co-founded SoftKey Software products, a software company in Ontario. It was known as The Learning Company after acquiring the original The Learning Company and taking its name. He sold the software to giant plotter manufacturers. Back in those days, if a business wanted to plot a graph with a multi-access plotter, his company had the controlling software. The Learning Company was the world's largest educational reference software company and was eventually sold for 4.2 billion dollars. He and his team realized they were rich, took a moment to internalize the fact and went back to work. That was the key, he says. He has had many many deals since then. “It’s not greed for capital, you don’t need that kind of money to live to be honest, you will definitely enjoy certain things, but it didn’t change our lives” says O’Leary. In fact he circles back to utilizing the time productively and how it has been used to help other entrepreneurs succeed. He believes that if anyone has made good money, they should pass it forward.Growing up, his mother imparted crucial financial wisdom, emphasizing the importance of living within one’s means. O’Leary advocates for saving 20 percent of the income, investing into the market over a 20 - 30 year span with an expected annual return of  6 - 8 percent. Following this disciplined approach, he envisions the potential of accumulated substantial sums, like a million and a half dollars, in the bank account upon retirement with an average income of $54, 000 in America. His relationship with money has always been being careful not to waste it. O’Leary gets into the nuances of saving money for people who are on a budget and who live paycheck to paycheck. He confidently says that he can walk around with a person for a day and show that they are wasting 15 percent of their money on unnecessary stuff.  Spending a lot of money on a daily basis on a sandwich, coffee, water bottle, a drink and many other things can add up to a lot and can be cut down by carrying them from home instead. He quotes that making a sandwich at home costs 99 cents against $15 for buying it. Especially for those working in metropolitan areas or starting to save their money, keeping a tab on  spending is crucial. On the other hand, to increase income, engaging in side hustles is advocated. O'Leary suggests numerous contemporary options, such as editing social media content, creating commercials, narrating corporate stories, and managing TikTok accounts for prominent media figures. These avenues present diverse opportunities for individuals seeking additional sources of income. Either upscaling or putting one’s skills to work is a smart way to make an extra buck. He clearly doesn't believe in the nine to five job market anymore, since his team is spread all over the world and are very productive. That’s the new economy, he says. O’Leary claims that hiring good talent leads to more talent and he says that he follows a ‘slow to hire and quick to fire’ strategy in his companies since it hinders if everyone is not moving in the same direction. He is very proud of his extremely talented team. He is generous in sharing the profits and everybody gets rewarded and recognised for their contribution. He acknowledges that running millions of dollars worth of advisory business is not easy and he cannot do it alone. He recently became an Emirati citizen, because he wants to do a lot of work in Abu Dhabi. He thinks that Abu Dhabi is going to become the capital of the capital in terms of business in the next ten years and he wants to be part of it.
doc: article/Kevin O’Leary_ Business tips from the Shark.docx

name: Laura Kaslow
title: Who knew, creating ornaments out of maps could pay up for the horses! Maptometry by Laura Kaslow
story: Laura Kaslow, a remarkable artist, loving mom, and a beautiful soul, has carved a unique niche in the art world with her creation - Maptometry. For the past six years, Kaslow has been transforming antique optometric lenses from eye doctors into captivating pieces of art using recycled and repurposed antique and vintage maps. Kaslow's artistic journey began with a purpose close to her heart – funding her daughter's Equine therapy. Facing the financial challenge of insurance not covering this therapy for her daughter, who battled the connective tissue disorder Ehlers-Danlos, Kaslow sought an unconventional path. Equine therapy, known for promoting physical, occupational, and emotional growth, became a beacon of hope for her family. This therapy, involving activities with horses, was recommended by her daughter's geneticist. To fund this essential therapy, Kaslow ingeniously fused antique maps into optometric lenses. This not only transformed maps into beautiful artifacts but also provided economic sustainability for her family. It's a testament to Kaslow's dedication and creativity that she found a way to navigate the challenging landscape of the art industry. Wearing a pendant crafted from an 1875 French lens embedded with a 1981 Crater Lake, Oregon map from the National Geographic student atlas, Kaslow emphasizes the authenticity of her art, I use real maps, I don't photocopy them. There is history, there is recycling, she asserts. Her commitment to recycling and repurposing aligns with her belief in the importance of exposure to art. According to Kaslow, art broadens perspectives and encourages people to see things differently, a sentiment that fuels her passion. Despite the economic challenges inherent in the art industry, Kaslow's success is not just measured in financial terms but in the connections she forges. I get to meet nice people, she shares with a genuine smile. Her journey goes beyond creating beautiful art; it's about sharing and listening to stories. When individuals pick a map for their ornament, they often share their experiences or memories attached to that specific location. Kaslow's Maptometry has become a platform where personal stories intertwine with her artistic creations. Each ornament crafted by Kaslow is a testament to her artistic vision and a tangible representation of the histories encapsulated in the real maps she employs. Her dedication to economic sustainability, the well-being of her daughter, and her belief in the transformative power of art make Laura Kaslow's journey truly remarkable. Maptometry not only enriches homes with unique pieces but also stands as a witness to the resilience and creativity of an artist who turned challenges into a canvas for inspiration.

name: Philip Reynolds
title: Embracing Authenticity Phillip Reynolds' Journey of Compassion and Resilience in Mental Health Care
story: In the realm of behavioral and mental health, Phillip Reynolds stands as a beacon of empathy and resilience, with nearly two decades of experience under his belt. Armed with a Master of Social Work degree and an LCSW-C certification, Phillip brings a unique blend of clinical expertise and genuine care to his work, leaving a trail of positive impact wherever he goes. Phillip's journey began at a counseling and learning center in Watts, traversing through West LA and eventually settling in Washington DC, where he found not just a job, but a home and a partner. His career path meandered through various roles, from residential treatment programs to private practice, until the pandemic forced a pivot, leading him to launch PHP - Partial Hospitalization Patients and IOP - Intensive Outpatients programs in the Virginia area while continuing his private practice and executive coaching endeavors. In his roles as both a private practitioner and an area executive director at Embark Behavioral Health, Phillip's focus remains unwavering: to provide personalized and effective treatments while overseeing the operations of mental health clinics in the District of Columbia, Maryland, Verginia area. For Phillip, building relationships lies at the core of his practice, whether it's fostering connections with clients in therapy sessions or cultivating a supportive network within his team. He emphasizes the importance of genuine care and compassion, recognizing that every individual seeks to be seen and valued. In the face of challenges, Phillip adopts a pragmatic approach, acknowledging that hurdles are an inherent part of growth and progress. He surrounds himself with a diverse array of advisors and collaborators, embracing the wisdom of those who bring different perspectives to the table. As he navigates the complexities of mental health care, Phillip remains anchored in his faith and his authenticity, refusing to compromise his values for societal approval. He encourages open dialogue and transparency, recognizing that vulnerability is a gateway to meaningful connections and personal growth.Amidst the pressures of social media and societal expectations, Phillip stays true to himself, embracing humor as a coping mechanism and a source of resilience. He acknowledges that while not everyone may resonate with his approach, staying genuine is non-negotiable for him.Phillip feels immense joy when clients express gratitude for the impact he’s had on their lives. Hearing from former clients years later, seeing their growth and success, is incredibly rewarding. Phillip sees it as a privilege to be part of their journey.Looking back on his journey, Phillip finds solace in the laughter that spread through every aspect of his life. Humor, he believes, is not just a means of lightening the mood, but a powerful tool for navigating life's challenges with grace and resilience.Phillip Reynolds' story is one of perseverance, compassion, and unwavering authenticity. Through his work, he embodies the belief that genuine connections and a touch of humor can transform lives and inspire hope in the face of adversity. As he continues to chart new territories in the field of mental health, Phillip remains committed to fostering a world where everyone feels seen, heard, and valued.

name: Stephen Sindoni
title: Key to success - Steering an enterprise and life from a Military perspective.
story: How does an entrepreneur, a retired military officer and a dad juggle work, business and home, that too with a smile on his face? Stick with me to find out, since I spoke with Stephen Sindoni on some of his life experiences. After traveling across the nation and abroad, Sindoni has learned a thing or two. He shares how he applies his learnings from the Navy in his day to day activities. After retiring from an honorable role in the U.S. Navy, Sindoni works as a systems sales engineer at Cubic Mission & Performance Solutions and also runs a karate studio, USSD Irvine 2, under the umbrella of United Studios of Self Defense. USSD is the largest martial arts school in North America and has a tradition of over 60 years of martial arts. The studio encourages all ages to learn self defense. It has students ranging from three or four years old to 60+ year old fitness and discipline enthusiasts. The students learn a unique system of Kempo-style martial arts from the finest instructors in the United States. A standardized syllabus is followed by USSD across the nation for various belt rankings. Starting from an entry level White belt, students progress to Yellow, Orange, Purple, Blue, intermediate level Blue with Green stripe, Green, another intermediate level Green with Brown stripe, three degrees of Brown belt and they are finally ready to become a student of Martial Arts once they earn their First Degree Black belt. Earning a First Degree Black Belt typically takes 10 to 12 years of dedication. Martial arts is an important skill for everyone to have under their belt. It teaches you discipline, self defense, and allows you to understand yourself better. Moreover, it’s a fun way to burn some calories! Sparring, or controlled fighting, helps you understand what kind of fighter you are and helps you predict other people’s movements, making you better at defending yourself, needless to mention improving your stamina, reaction time, and strategic thinking skills! Forms help you understand how your body works and what feels most comfortable for you. For instance, are you a light and graceful crane or a powerful tiger? An agile leopard or an accurate snake? Or are you a mix of everything like a twisting, twirling dragon? Martial arts also help you learn to focus and stay calm and collected. Meditation is part of martial arts and helps strengthen your mind as well as your body. There is a united teaching method across the company and the founder, 10th Degree black belt, Professor Charles Mattera, put together a manual to help the Senseis understand the material so they can deliver the finest instructions. The manual catalogs every micro detail that is needed for effective training. The uniformity plays an evident role when the students compete in tournaments, while practicing at other dojos or while attending seminars. Being in the military, Sindoni has learned that nothing comes easy and everything takes a certain amount of work to be achieved. He swears by patience, perseverance, and hard work to succeed in any area. Having integrity to work with people of different backgrounds is necessary. “We all have similarities. We have differences too, but more similarities,” Sindoni says, and “it is important that we keep the fabric of family, friends, and business associates in line with our goals.” Nobody likes to be with people who constantly bring down others because they are in turmoil. Sindoni suggests that once people identify the challenges that surround them and take ownership of their lives, they will start to improve. He says this is the importance of the military and the importance of his karate studio. His goal and vision for the studio is to provide capability, hard work, challenges and team camaraderie, while encouraging independent spirit. To accommodate the above mentioned faculties, the karate studio offers a program called the Specialized Team of Role Models program STORM for short. It is a combination program like a team sport where you not only rely on yourself but you also rely on others to achieve better. He says that martial arts is like an open ended relationship with everybody yet challenging oneself to focus on self development as well as the karate school. Sindoni always liked martial arts, he cherished his juvenility, when he watched martial arts movies and played it out with friends. He got trained and joined the military in his early 20s. During his last year of active duty at the U.S. Navy, his daughter was one year into learning karate. When he returned home from Germany, retiring from the Military in 2015 and was working as a contractor for Jacob’s supporting the marine corps, he thought that karate should be cool to get back into shape. According to him, that was the best since he wouldn’t be as active as on duty and would miss both the physical and mental conditioning. Hence, martial arts was a good application. He eventually started a karate school under USSD, with a hope that he could provide his daughter an opportunity which he didn’t have growing up. As he hoped, his daughter achieved and earned her first-degree black belt at the age of 14. What we notice as we get older is that “it’s easy to walk away from things and just quit” and “If you have that easy way out of doing difficult things, that will always be your exit strategy,”  Sindoni briefly says. When it comes to doing regular tasks or jobs, one might feel like just disregarding and walking away, but it's important to realize that if we put in the time and effort and look by, it becomes easier and automatic. In the STORM program, “it’s different to be taught versus to teach the material.” He asserts. The students of this program can process and explain a procedure, movement or form which, in turn, reinforces and enhances their personal skill sets. Since the STORM team will be interacting with peers, students, and their students’ parents, it boosts their confidence to a great extent. That is a life skill which can be learned only through experience. At the same time, it also teaches them the value of charity. Around twice a year, USSD has a tournament hosted by one dojo. Students from all the participating dojos compete in forms, open forms, weapons, or sparring. Students can choose whichever categories they’d like to compete in and train for them in their own dojos. Tournaments are a great way for students to meet people from other studios. They can make friends, learn what other instructors are like, and see where they stand compared to their peers. Tournaments also allow students to better themselves by learning from their peers and higher ranks. Tournaments provide exposure to different performance styles, people, and instructors. They also boost confidence after the student has competed. Even if they don’t win gold, it is a learning experience and a fun time. Sindoni has traveled to different parts of the country and to different parts of the world such as Germany, Japan, Iraq, Afghanistan, China, Taiwan, Korea, many parts of Europe and he says that it doesn't matter where people come from, he has seen that everyday people want to feel blessed for their talent, their relationships and they are driven by specifically that. Because we come from different backgrounds and traditions, sometimes, the things that are said on social media are hurtful.  He hopes the studio has a sense of family, an environment that is safe and the students can share their views and not feel offended. “The students have a feeling that they belong to something which is bigger than themselves,” Sindoni hopes.“Females today are much more challenged in an environment and they need a voice,” he expresses. It is very important that “men need a voice, women and kids need a voice” but “they need to be dependent on themselves” they need to rely on themselves and know who they are and what’s important to them. Martial arts is about testing people and pushing them to higher levels similar to the workforce and the military special operations, where they apply themselves into achieving their best. That’s how leaders are created. Two years ago, due to many factors, his karate studio underwent a radical transformation. The management, coaching staff, and students went through change. Change is something many of us are not comfortable with, or worse, do not know how to handle. However, Sindoni came out of the other end of the change and he is full of gratitude and has a sense of relief. He says he was able to find a balance during those unsteady times because of support from other business owners of the studios, the founder of USSD, his family, friends, students and parents; he mentions that he couldn’t have handled it all by himself. Since the studio was functioning well, he envisioned growth and expansion for the team. He had plans of opening another studio, but he wasn’t able to, due to all the sudden changes that he had to deal with.However, the situation taught him a lot. He learned that business is fragile. A new business or a startup has a two to five year window for it to stabilize, where it has a very high fail ratio. Evidently, the more support and resources you have available to help you to get through, the easier it gets to make right decisions. “Did I make the right decisions everyday? Well, I don’t know, but the key foundation of what’s important to me is the way I run the studio” Sindoni explains. He makes sure that the studio is professional, safe, and clean and it is important that the parents also notice that. A lot of effort that goes into maintaining it. Running a business in Irvine means that you have to be competitive, ensure that you stay in business, and provide a service that is beneficial to all ages which enables them to grow and progress. As Sindoni quotes, “Life is like that, the little things we need to do in life. If we do them every day, we will realize that we have accomplished big things at the end of the day.” Everything takes a certain amount of effort, persistence and a great deal of will to pursue what you started and your goals can be achieved with a stride.

name: Terry Hairston
title: Transforming Lives: The Inspiring Journey of Terence Lamont Hairston
story: In a world often overshadowed by self-interest, Terry Hairston stands as a beacon of hope, tirelessly working to uplift those in need and inspire positive change. From his remarkable achievements as a professional bodybuilder to his unwavering commitment to helping individuals recover from addiction, Terry's journey is a testament to the power of persistence, compassion, and the unwavering human spirit.Terry's story is one of transformation, both personal and communal. His journey began amidst the challenges of growing up in an environment plagued by alcoholism and drug abuse. Despite facing hardships, Terry made a conscious choice to break free from the cycle of self-destruction. His decision to leave behind a life of selling drugs and pursue a path of health and wellness marked the beginning of a remarkable transformation.Through dedication and perseverance, Terry found solace and purpose in fitness. He embraced bodybuilding as not only a means of physical strength but also as a journey of self-discovery and redemption. As he honed his physique and achieved success in competitions, Terry realized the profound impact that fitness could have on one's life. However, it was a personal tragedy that truly shaped Terry's life's purpose. The loss of his brother to alcoholism served as a catalyst for Terry to channel his energy towards helping others struggling with addiction. In honor of his brother, Terry founded The Tracy Dion Hairston Foundation, a beacon of hope for those seeking recovery and a path to a healthier life.The foundation's mission is clear: to provide support, guidance, and resources to individuals battling addiction, empowering them to choose a path of sobriety and wellness. Through a combination of fitness training, mentorship, and community support, Terry and his team work tirelessly to help individuals reclaim their lives and find fulfillment beyond addiction. At the heart of Terry's approach is the belief that true transformation begins from within. By addressing the underlying trauma and internal struggles that often fuel addiction, Terry helps individuals develop the resilience and strength needed to overcome adversity. Through personalized training programs, counseling, and ongoing support, Terry guides his clients on a journey of healing and self-discovery. What sets Terry's foundation apart is its holistic approach to recovery. Recognizing that addiction is not just a physical ailment but a complex interplay of psychological, emotional, and social factors, Terry integrates fitness, therapy, and community support to address the root causes of addiction. By fostering a sense of belonging and purpose, Terry empowers individuals to build a life free from the grips of addiction. Terry's dedication to his foundation is matched only by his passion for fitness. As the owner of multiple fitness centers, Terry not only promotes physical health but also serves as a role model for his clients. His mantra ‘Lead By Example’ shows his commitment to leading by example underscores his belief that true leadership is rooted in empathy, integrity, and compassion. As Terry continues to expand the reach of his foundation and empower individuals to lead healthier, more fulfilling lives, his message resonates far beyond the confines of a gym or recovery center. It is a testament to the transformative power of compassion, resilience, and the unwavering human spirit. In a world often defined by division and despair, Terry Hairston stands as a shining example of what it means to make a difference. Through his tireless efforts, he reminds us that no matter how daunting the challenge, no matter how dark the night may seem, there is always hope for a brighter tomorrow.

name: Catherine Hahn
title: Crafting careers, transforming lives and building communities - A journey from resume writing to building leadership.
author: Jayanthi Krishna
story: Career coach, personal brand builder, Catherine Hahn, focuses on career development. She coaches anyone starting from high school students to CEO level. From job identification to career fulfillment to change in career. She believes that everyone has a career passion and she wants to help people identify and nurture their passion. After graduating from UCLA Hahn joined Getty Museum as an intern curator, although she loved to study history and classical civilisation, she was not fulfilled with her internship.  Then she moved on to work as an executive administrative assistant, to her luck, she got fired in the first week. Though it was very disappointing, Hahn gathered herself and looked up on the internet which was fairly new during 1999. She joined her first job at Mason Concepts in the sales department. She figured out how important it is to be prepared for the job. Being prepared is indeed crucial for success and efficiency in the workplace. Later when worked as a recruiter in an IT company, ironically the company’s management decided to part ways and dissolved the firm. Hahn started working for one of the business owners and specialized in Government recruiting for the City of Los Angeles. Without even a hint of imposter syndrome, Hahn tried to grab everything that came her way and she accelerated her career to new heights, by recruiting in various departments like Health care, Legal, Marketing, and many other sectors across the board. That’s an impressive level of confidence. She was going great guns for someone who was only 22 years old. While recruiting for Government sectors, she discovered that the candidates had to have a certain parameter on the resume that flows into the job which supports them to land a job. So she started helping her clients write an effective resume. Hahn was good at writing because of her degree in college and found out that resume writing was a separate field she could pursue. Since she was evidently getting busy at home with young children, she needed a job she could operate remotely. Once she got her foot in the door, she started her own company called Affordable Resume Coach. Hahn is a certified resume writer and an interview professional. Started advising her clients on career opportunities alongside writing resumes, that segwayed to many other arenas. During the pandemic of 2020,  as her parents wanted to retire from their real estate business, Hahn along with her younger brother, took over the business and continued to run the business. On the other hand her teenage daughters were emotionally hit by the lockdown and were looking for an opportunity to make use of their time. One day while watching the news, they thought they could start a small library for those who are interested in reading books. To encourage reading habits, Hahn started mentoring her daughters and their two friends who started a book club called ‘Dynamite Book Club’. The club organically grew and “it was almost meant to be that way, because they were approached by random people who asked them what they were doing” says Hahn, people started showing interest in their project and supported them. Started at her house with their personal library, gradually they started putting up book boxes for people to borrow and donate books. The kids felt purposeful as they continued. “My goal in life is to find everyone’s purpose as a career coach” fondly, shares Hahn. As a mentor, she started creating projects for students who could participate as volunteers and get hands-on experience.  The book club has grown to 15 libraries and 3 more in the pipeline to go, encouraging many students now. Hahn guides and mentors students who approach for assistance. She takes great care of the entire project that the students propose to work on. “Don’t fear asking” is her slogan for anyone that she works with, “worst case, if you get rejected you move on to the next” advises Hahn. Always be prone to saying yes, since you do not know what’s in store for you. Evidently her pet peeve is instant gratification, she insists that everyone has to work toward their goal and they will certainly achieve it, but without the efforts, nothing’s going to happen. Talking about facing the fear and handling the fear factor, Hahn says that when she was young she herself used to be an introvert to a large extent, so much that she tried to avoid going to church due to social anxiety, because she had to face many people instead she would hide in her house. She understands the new generation with similar issues. Hahn shares a few tips for especially students who are hesitant to take further steps.  When talking to someone, looking above their head instead of looking into their face helps. While addressing a large crowd just focus on your words and do not worry about the people, you can handle it much better. She talks about narrow focus - An attentional style in which an individual has the ability to concentrate on relevant stimuli and ignore other stimuli. For facing fear or a challenge, first of all make a list of things to do to tackle the situation and practice, then put up a confident persona and face the world. Speaking of failures and humiliation, she giggles remembering Aaliyah’s song title ‘Then dust yourself off and try again’ Hahn says, that’s it, “what if you failed in something, just pull yourself up and try again”, if you dwell on failure, you are only blocking yourself. “If you say ‘I can’ and ‘yes’, you are opening a world of opportunities beyond your imagination” As a career counselor it is very natural for her to speak about career plans, growth, and living a certain kind of lifestyle. She commonly finds that kids (especially college bound students) think that they have a lot of time! Hahn sits with them and starts to explain to them as: Think of yourself 10 from now, you’ll be an adult. Start taking charge of yourself from now; what do you want to achieve, what kind of lifestyle do you want to have? Chart out the necessary action items to maximize your time available considering the age of the student. One can choose when to start their adulthood if they have a clear cut plan of action, discipline and time management - reaching milestones as per plan is key for reaching goals. Sounds cliche, but time management is number one! One of the best tools is a vision board. It is a great aid that represents one’s goals and aspirations, and enhances visualization of your success. It is a personal and creative tool for manifesting aspirations and fostering a positive mindset. People will have the capacity to perform better and to their potential with guidance and a bit of handholding. Hahn takes pleasure in mentioning about one of her clients, who works as a radiology manager at a doctor’s office and he is all in for strategic growth. By both volunteering and as part of the workforce, he has been doing very well for himself, his podcast on cancer cure has been on top of the charts,  he also has designed a program for caregivers which is classified information. She coaches entrepreneurs on marketing strategies, business development, and enjoys a bit of property management as well. Hahn genuinely wants to encourage youngsters to create their own career path which leads them to a great lifestyle. Thinking back on her own career path, Hahn wishes that she had Catherine Hahn by her side to give the guidance and advice she gives her clients today. She thinks she should’ve explored more options and probably would have landed in the healthcare industry. Her biggest regret is that she should’ve continued her education to become a scientist and dietitian as her side gig. Hahn offers her clients a very broad spectrum of services from getting into a job, growth, change in career path, skill sets, long term strategic planning considering paying for mortgage and other loans, travel plans, though she doesn’t focus on finance as a financial advisor would, she helps her clients to plan their retirement, in terms of how much money one needs to feel fulfilled to retire and lead a comfortable life.  She doesn’t look at retirement as what happens when someone stops working, she looks at it more like a lifestyle aspect. Women should have a clear plan of what they want to do, how long do they want to take a break from their career in case they want to focus more on their personal and family life. Some of her clients intended to take a two year sabbatical, instead had to take a 10 year break. The Internet has a wide range of options for jobs like, return to work jobs, jobs for over 50s and getting back to college to refine their skills is a great investment, suggests Hahn. Consulting a career coach is the most effective and efficient way to restart or change a career path. Since Hahn wears so many hats, like a career counselor, mentor to volunteers, property management,  caregiver to her parents, social worker, and many more, she has to be on top of everything. Past two years she’s been maintaining a thorough planner after a friend advised her to have a planner. She chuckles and she credits her neuro diverse ADHD for her high energy in slaying the game. Writing things down has proved very helpful for her, a gratitude journal puts in the right mood for the day to handle it well. “I want the kids to give back to society, it’s good for the soul and their mental health, gives them a perspective.”  It is not just workforce volunteering but also for the community, which gives them hands-on experience, it expands the possibilities and creates a better generation. Hahn passionately shares “I’m hoping that one day even with one person at a time I’ll be able to help” that’s what she enjoys most about her role as a mentor and a coach. It’s important that everyone has a hobby to unwind and enjoy something other than a regular routine. Hahn has an interesting hobby of metal detection at the beach. She’s been lucky to find a silver ring in her exploration. She’s aiming to collect a lot more interesting pieces of metal at the beach. Hobbies help in taking a minute for yourself when constantly being busy. It’s good to be busy but it’s also important to take breaks and invest in oneself. Hahn has a very interesting approach which she has practiced with her clients who are seeking jobs. She has a unique program of ‘Brainstorming bubbles’ which actually helps in creating and building an extensive networking system. She highly recommends that students coming out of college should have internships or part time jobs lined up even before they get out of college. She is constantly looking for ways of helping out her clients and her volunteers so that they are benefited in more than one way. Exploring career options is crucial and she recommends volunteering at the workforce to get the practical feel of the job, environment and expectations of the industry. On the other hand, it helps in building a network.Which I feel is a privilege to have. Hahn jokingly says that engaging with random people on social media could lead to a lot of potential progress.In closing, Catherine Hahn's journey from intern curator to CEO-level career coach exemplifies the power of resilience, adaptability, and relentless pursuit of one's passion. Her dedication to helping others identify and nurture their career passions is truly inspiring. Through her diverse experiences and unwavering commitment, she has not only transformed her own career but has also positively impacted the lives of countless individuals, guiding them towards fulfilling and purposeful paths. As we reflect on her remarkable story, let us embrace her advice to fearlessly pursue our goals, to embrace failure as a stepping stone to success, and to always strive for personal and professional growth. Catherine Hahn's journey serves as a beacon of hope and motivation for anyone navigating their own career path, reminding us that with perseverance and the right guidance, anything is possible.
